text: The surgeon attempts to elicit the rectal prolapse by instructing the patient to cough, observing any visible protrusion or movement of the rectal tissue.

The surgeon prepares for a stapled transanal rectal resection using a circular stapler device. The stay sutures are placed on each side, and the stapler is closed with the orange mark in the green firing zone. The surgeon checks the vaginal wall to ensure it is not entrapped before firing the stapler. Due to the significant prolapse, the device is opened and removed carefully.

The surgeon opens the circular stapler device and removes it under direct vision, revealing the full-thickness section of the rectum with visible parietal fat. The surgeon identifies a tissue bridge and prepares to break it.

The surgeon carefully removes scales from the posterior rectum, ensuring the lumen remains intact and protected. The circular stapler device is positioned to target the prolapsed rectum at the nine o'clock position.

The surgeon positions the circular stapler on the scale, achieving a full-thickness resection of the rectum. The resection reveals parietal fat surrounding the excised tissue. The surgeon prepares to proceed with the resection at the three o'clock position.
The surgeon places a stay suture at the six o'clock position on the highest point of the rectal prolapse, achieving full thickness resection with visible parietal fat surrounding the specimen.

The surgeon observes the surgical field, noting no residual prolapse except anteriorly and laterally. The lateral residual prolapse is removed, revealing a full thickness resection with visible parietal fat.

The surgeon addresses the residual prolapse component on the left side of the patient, placing a stay suture and using a sixth stapler to excise the full thickness section. The parietal fat is visible, and upon inspection, no further prolapse is observed.

The surgeon inserts a gauze piece into the rectum, applying pressure to check for any residual prolapse. Despite the patient coughing and straining, no prolapse is observed anteriorly, laterally, or on the lateral wall.
